title: Berry-Phase-Induced Chirality in Thermodynamics
abstract: Geometric phases are foundational to isolated quantum systems, yet their thermodynamic role in open systems remains unrevealed Developing a dissipative adiabatic perturbation expansion, we discover a Berry-phase-induced chiral work difference that survives decoherence. This chirality evolves from an interferometric thermodynamic Aharonov-Bohm effect in the unitary regime to a fringe-free signal in the dissipative regime. We illustrate this framework in a two-level system and assess its experimental feasibility. Our findings clarify the role of quantum geometry in the geometric formulation of thermodynamics.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the Berry phase induces a chiral asymmetry in the work performed on an open quantum system. While quantum interference fringes disappear in the presence of strong decoherence, a robust, 'fringe-free' chiral work signal remains. This finding clarifies how quantum geometry influences the geometric formulation of thermodynamics in dissipative environments.
main_problem: Investigating the thermodynamic role of the Berry phase in open quantum systems and determining if geometric signatures persist or are erased by decoherence.
main_result: The authors discover a Berry-phase-induced chiral work difference that survives decoherence, transitioning from an oscillatory 'interferometric' signal in the unitary regime to a robust, fringe-free signal in the dissipative regime.
method: The study employs a Dissipative Adiabatic Perturbation Expansion (DAPE) framework to expand off-diagonal coherences in terms of an adiabaticity parameter.
model_or_system: An open quantum system (specifically a two-level system) driven by time-dependent control parameters and weakly coupled to a thermal bath, following a Born-Markov master equation.
key_observables: Average work (W), chiral work difference (delta W), and thermodynamic length (L).
important_parameters: Adiabaticity parameter (epsilon), driving frequency (Omega), relaxation/dephasing rates (gamma), and the Berry phase (Phi).
main_assumptions: The system is in the slow-driving (adiabatic) regime, the Lamb shift is neglected, and population differences are treated as approximately constant to leading order.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of geometric phases in thermodynamics, develops the DAPE framework, decomposes work into metric and Berry-phase contributions, demonstrates the discovery of chiral work, and validates the theory using a two-level system and NMR-like parameters.
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental link between quantum geometry and irreversible thermodynamics, showing that the thermal bath can actually act as a mechanism to encode geometric information into measurable, robust thermodynamic quantities.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
